output.summary_final: {"title": "Building Trails That Last: Skills, Setup, and Maintenance for Mountain Bikers", "bullets": ["Speed gains come from braking earlier and smoother rather than taking bigger risks — a counterintuitive but reliable principle for riders at any level.", "Cornering on berms requires eyes focused through the exit, centered hips, and pressure that builds gradually rather than spiking abruptly.", "Tire pressure and casing choice typically have more impact on feel and traction than minor suspension adjustments, making them the first setup variable to dial in.", "For climbs, maintaining a consistent cadence reduces drivetrain load and makes it easier to manage small, sudden changes in traction.", "In wet conditions, the most valuable skill is decision-making — knowing when to back off protects both rider traction and trail surface integrity.", "Well-drained trails stay rideable longer because intact soil structure resists rutting; trail design and rider restraint both contribute to longevity.", "A reliable maintenance routine is deliberately unglamorous: check bolts, measure chain wear, clean stanchions, and inspect brake pads on a regular schedule.", "Most mystery creaks are resolved by cleaning contact surfaces, re-greasing threads, and re-torquing fasteners to spec — no new parts required.", "Skill development accelerates when riders isolate one trail segment and change only one variable at a time, then record observations to iterate methodically."]}

{"title": "On-Call That Doesn't Break People", "bullets": ["A well-designed on-call rotation keeps wake-ups rare; when pages do happen, the correct response should be obvious and well-documented, reducing cognitive load on the engineer on duty.", "Alerting should be strictly action-oriented: if no one can take an immediate action in response to an alert, it probably shouldn't page at all.", "Healthy systems treat failures as expected events, containing them through timeouts, retries, and clear ownership rather than relying on heroics.", "Error budgets only work when they actively change team behavior and planning decisions; error budget charts that nobody consults during planning provide no real value.", "Architecture decisions are fundamentally tradeoffs—cost vs. reliability, speed vs. correctness, autonomy vs. consistency—and should be evaluated explicitly rather than defaulted into.", "Writing decisions down transforms disagreement into collaboration, because teams can critique assumptions on paper rather than arguing with each other directly. A strong RFC includes context, constraints, options, and a recommendation with explicit risks.", "Secrets handling is strongest when tokens are short-lived, narrowly scoped, and rotated through automation rather than managed manually.", "Security works best when the secure path is also the easiest path, not when compliance depends on shame or individual heroics. Lightweight threat modeling—identifying assets, entry points, trust boundaries, and likely abuse cases—supports this goal."]}

{"title": "Wreck Diving Fundamentals: Staying Calm, Safe, and Prepared Below the Surface", "bullets": ["Calm is a core safety asset underwater: slow breathing conserves gas and keeps decision-making deliberate, making the calmest diver often the safest diver.", "Most underwater stress stems from surprise combined with lack of rehearsal, not from the inherent difficulty of the problem — practice transforms alarming situations into manageable ones.", "For navigation, always pair a primary reference with a backup, such as compass heading plus depth, or reef contour plus current direction, to maintain orientation reliably.", "A backup light is more than redundancy — knowing you have a fallback option actively changes your mental state and reduces anxiety during a dive.", "Silt is a serious hazard inside wrecks: what feels like a clear hallway can become a disorienting blank room in seconds if you fin too hard.", "Good buoyancy control serves a dual purpose — it protects fragile environments and simultaneously reduces cognitive task load, freeing attention for navigation and photography.", "Neutral buoyancy drills such as hovering and slow fin pivots should be practiced regularly, as they make everything else easier when conditions deteriorate.", "Dive plans work best when kept simple, shared with your buddy, and built around clear turn pressures and exit routes — and the most valuable skill is the willingness to call a dive early without ego."]}

{"title": "Underwater Photography: Lighting, Positioning, and Workflow Habits", "bullets": ["The camera is rarely the limiting factor in underwater photography — lighting and positioning are the primary determinants of image quality, making those skills the most worthwhile to develop.", "Adjusting strobe angle even slightly can dramatically reduce backscatter by moving the light beam away from the lens axis, a simple but high-impact technique for cleaner underwater shots.", "Grids are less about creating soft light and more about controlling spill — they keep light off areas that should remain dark, giving photographers precise control over the frame.", "In mixed lighting situations, identify the dominant color temperature first and correct everything else relative to that anchor decision rather than trying to balance all sources simultaneously.", "Practicing with a single light teaches more than working with a complex multi-light kit, because it forces a deeper understanding of placement and the relationship between light and shadow.", "Every light in a setup should have a clear, explainable purpose — if you cannot articulate why a light is there, it likely does not need to be there yet.", "Documentary and long-term projects depend on consistent small habits: same-day file labeling, caption writing, and regular backups prevent memory gaps and catastrophic data loss.", "Great images stem from a clear concept and a simple, consistently executed setup — shortcuts in planning or workflow tend to undermine both the creative and archival integrity of a project."]}

{"title": "Index Investing Without the Myths", "bullets": ["Risk is not the same as volatility. True risk is the chance of failing to meet your goals or suffering a permanent loss of capital — a distinction that should shape every investment decision.", "Index investing works because it reduces costs and eliminates decision errors, not because it predicts future market performance. Managing what you can control is the core advantage.", "Fees compound quietly against you; lowering costs is functionally equivalent to increasing returns without taking on any additional risk.", "The hardest part of investing is behavioral: staying consistent when headlines are alarming and portfolios are down. Good frameworks must be simple enough to survive your worst moods.", "Drawdowns carry both a math and an emotional cost. A 50% loss requires a 100% gain just to break even, which can dramatically extend timelines and force poor decisions.", "Position sizing rules are essential — they prevent a single high-conviction idea from becoming a portfolio-ending mistake.", "Diversification only works when assets have genuinely different return drivers. Different tickers that move together in a crisis provide little real protection.", "Real estate appears stable until leverage, vacancy, and maintenance problems arrive simultaneously. A useful stress test: can you absorb higher rates, longer vacancies, and unexpected repairs without panic selling?"]}
